stem: A 31-year-old woman attends her general practice for routine contraceptive review. She has a Mirena 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system that was inserted three years ago for contraception and management of menorrhagia. She has been amenorrhoeic for the past 18 months, is happy with the device, has no pelvic pain, no abnormal bleeding, and her last cervical screening test was normal 12 months ago. She asks the practice nurse how much longer the current device will remain effective for contraception before it needs to be replaced. She has no plans to conceive in the next five years and is in a monogamous relationship.

data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 24. Speculum: Mirena threads visible at cervical os, no discharge.
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening test normal 12 months ago. No further investigations performed today.

lead_in: What is the licensed duration of contraceptive effectiveness for the Mirena 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system in Australia?

options.text: 3 years
options.rationale: 3 years is the licensed duration for the Kyleena and Jaydess lower-dose levonorgestrel systems and the Implanon NXT etonogestrel implant, not Mirena (TGA/AMH).

options.text: 5 years
options.rationale: 5 years was the prior TGA-licensed duration for Mirena but was extended to 8 years for contraception following updated efficacy data (TGA Product Information Mirena, 2023).

options.text: 8 years
options.rationale: The Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS is now licensed in Australia for up to 8 years of contraception following TGA approval of extended use; AMH and Family Planning Australia have adopted this duration (TGA PI Mirena; AMH 2024).

options.text: 10 years
options.rationale: 10 years is the typical effective duration of the copper IUD (e.g. Multiload Cu375, TT380 Standard), not the levonorgestrel system (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: 12 years
options.rationale: No intrauterine contraceptive is licensed in Australia for 12 years; this duration is not supported by TGA product information for any device (TGA).
source_refs: TGA Australian Register of Therapeutic Goods

explanation.summary: The Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS is licensed in Australia for 8 years of contraception (extended from 5 years in 2023). It remains licensed for 5 years when used for endometrial protection in MHT and for treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding.
key_points: Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS: 8 years for contraception (TGA 2023)
Mirena: 5 years for HMB and for endometrial protection on MHT
Kyleena 19.5 mg LNG-IUS: 5 years contraception
Implanon NXT etonogestrel implant: 3 years
Copper IUD (TT380): 10 years
explanation.pearls: Different indications carry different licensed durations for the same device. The contraceptive extension to 8 years does not automatically apply to HMB or MHT endometrial protection.

stem: A 24-year-old woman attends a sexual health clinic 30 hours after a single episode of condom failure with her regular partner. Her last menstrual period began 12 days ago and her usual cycle is 28 days. She is otherwise well, on no regular medications, has no allergies, BMI 23, no contraindications to oestrogen, and is concerned about preventing pregnancy. She asks the clinician which form of emergency contraception is most effective at preventing pregnancy from this single act of unprotected intercourse. She would accept any method, including a same-day intrauterine device if it is the most effective option available.

data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 23. Abdomen soft, no tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which method of emergency contraception is the most effective at preventing pregnancy after a single episode of unprotected intercourse?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg oral, single dose
options.rationale: Oral levonorgestrel reduces pregnancy risk by approximately 60-85% and is the least effective of the three established EC options (Family Planning Australia; FSRH 2023).

options.text: Ulipristal acetate 30 mg oral, single dose
options.rationale: Ulipristal is more effective than levonorgestrel particularly in the 72-120 hour window and around ovulation, but is less effective than the copper IUD (Cochrane 2017).

options.text: Copper intrauterine device inserted within 5 days
options.rationale: The copper IUD is the most effective form of emergency contraception with a failure rate well under 1% and is the only EC method that also provides ongoing contraception (Family Planning Australia; Cochrane 2017).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive Yuzpe regimen
options.rationale: The Yuzpe regimen is less effective than levonorgestrel and causes more nausea; it is no longer recommended when other options are available (FSRH).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system inserted within 5 days
options.rationale: The LNG-IUS is not currently a recommended emergency contraceptive method in Australian guidance; the copper IUD remains the IUD of choice for EC (Family Planning Australia).

explanation.summary: Ranking of emergency contraception efficacy (most to least): copper IUD > ulipristal acetate > levonorgestrel > Yuzpe. The copper IUD has a failure rate under 1% and also provides ongoing contraception for up to 10 years.
key_points: Cu-IUD: most effective EC, within 120 hours, ongoing contraception
Ulipristal 30 mg: up to 120 hours, single dose
Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg: up to 96 hours (licensed 72 h)
Avoid concurrent ulipristal and progestogen contraception for 5 days
explanation.pearls: Offer the copper IUD first to any woman who would accept it. BMI > 26 reduces oral EC efficacy and shifts the balance further toward Cu-IUD.

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman presents at 9 weeks gestation with three weeks of progressively worsening nausea and vomiting. She vomits six to ten times daily and is unable to keep down most fluids. She has lost 3.5 kg since her booking visit (5% of pre-pregnancy weight). She has no abdominal pain, no fever, no diarrhoea, and no urinary symptoms. Her PUQE score is 14. Her booking ultrasound at 8 weeks showed a single intrauterine pregnancy with appropriate growth and a normal cardiac activity. She has tried small frequent meals, ginger and pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily without benefit.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/68 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, T 36.7 degC. Postural drop 15 mmHg.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 seconds, abdomen soft and non-tender.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: ketones 3+, SG 1.030. Na 134, K 3.3, urea 7.8 mmol/L. Beta-hCG 78,000 IU/L. TSH 0.8 mIU/L. Ultrasound: single live intrauterine pregnancy.

lead_in: After IV rehydration with thiamine cover, which is the most appropriate first-line antiemetic to commence?

options.text: Doxylamine 12.5 mg with pyridoxine 25 mg orally up to four times daily
options.rationale: Doxylamine-pyridoxine is the recommended first-line pharmacological antiemetic in Australian guidance for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis after failure of pyridoxine alone (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ondansetron 8 mg orally three times daily
options.rationale: Ondansetron is effective but is reserved for second-line use; first-trimester use carries a small absolute increase in oral clefts and cardiac defects, so doxylamine-pyridoxine is preferred first (RANZCOG; TGA).

options.text: Metoclopramide 10 mg IV three times daily
options.rationale: Metoclopramide is a reasonable second-line option but carries extrapyramidal risk; first-line is doxylamine-pyridoxine (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Prochlorperazine 12.5 mg IM every 6 hours
options.rationale: Prochlorperazine is second- or third-line; phenothiazines may cause sedation and extrapyramidal effects (RANZCOG).

options.text: Methylprednisolone 16 mg orally three times daily
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are reserved for refractory hyperemesis after failure of multiple antiemetics and should be avoided before 10 weeks gestation due to a small cleft risk (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: First-line antiemetic for hyperemesis after pyridoxine alone has failed is doxylamine-pyridoxine. Ondansetron, metoclopramide and prochlorperazine are second-line. Steroids are reserved for refractory cases.
key_points: PUQE score quantifies severity
Hyperemesis: dehydration, ketonuria, >5% weight loss
Always give thiamine before glucose-containing fluids
Check TSH (gestational thyrotoxicosis often resolves)
explanation.pearls: If symptoms began after 10 weeks or there is abdominal pain, fever, headache or focal neurology, look for an alternative diagnosis.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 9 weeks and 4 days gestation by certain LMP attends the early pregnancy unit with a 24-hour history of fresh red vaginal bleeding and mild cramping. The bleeding has settled and she has passed some small clots at home. She has no fever, no shoulder tip pain and is haemodynamically stable. Her previous pregnancy ended in a term vaginal birth without complications and she did not require anti-D in that pregnancy. At booking she was confirmed Rh(D) negative with a negative antibody screen. The TVUS today shows a single intrauterine gestational sac with a fetal pole and cardiac activity appropriate for dates. The decision is made to manage expectantly and follow up in one week.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Speculum: small amount of dark blood, cervical os closed.
data_table.Investigations: Blood group O Rh(D) negative, antibody screen negative at booking and today. Beta-hCG 42,000 IU/L.

lead_in: What is the recommended anti-D immunoglobulin dose, if any, in this Australian clinical setting?

options.text: No anti-D is indicated at this gestation regardless of bleeding
options.rationale: Australian guidance (NBA, RANZCOG) recommends considering anti-D for sensitising events from 6 weeks gestation when there is bleeding, although the evidence is weaker before 12 weeks.

options.text: Anti-D 250 IU IM as a single dose
options.rationale: For sensitising events before 12 weeks (including threatened miscarriage with bleeding in a viable pregnancy), the Australian dose is 250 IU anti-D IM, given within 72 hours (NBA; RANZCOG).

options.text: Anti-D 625 IU IM as a single dose
options.rationale: 625 IU is the standard Australian dose for sensitising events at or after 12 weeks and for routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks, not for first-trimester events (NBA).

options.text: Anti-D 1500 IU IM as a single dose
options.rationale: 1500 IU is the UK routine antenatal dose, not used in the Australian schedule (BCSH 2014; NBA differs).

options.text: Withhold anti-D until Kleihauer testing confirms a fetomaternal haemorrhage
options.rationale: Kleihauer is not required for sensitising events before 20 weeks; anti-D should be given promptly without waiting (NBA).

explanation.summary: Australian anti-D dosing differs from the UK. Before 12 weeks: 250 IU for sensitising events. From 12 weeks: 625 IU for sensitising events and for routine prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks.
key_points: AU first trimester sensitising event: 250 IU IM
AU >=12 weeks sensitising event or routine RAADP at 28 and 34 weeks: 625 IU IM
Kleihauer required after 20 weeks to size FMH and guide additional anti-D
Anti-D must be given within 72 hours of the event
explanation.pearls: Memorise 250/625 IU as the Australian first/second-trimester pair. UK uses 250/1500 IU - different country, different dose.

sources.label: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

subtopic: GBS screening - timing of universal swab
stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 woman attends her midwife appointment at 34 weeks and 2 days gestation. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Her two prior births were uncomplicated term vaginal deliveries; neither neonate had early-onset GBS disease. Her booking bloods showed Hb 118 g/L, blood group A Rh positive, negative antibody screen, and negative HIV, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, syphilis and rubella-susceptibility status. Her 28-week OGTT was normal. She has had no preterm contractions, no rupture of membranes, no fever and no urinary tract infection this pregnancy. The local maternity service uses a universal culture-based screening pathway for group B streptococcus rather than a risk-factor pathway.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height 34 cm. Cephalic presentation. Fetal heart rate 142 bpm.
data_table.Investigations: Antenatal bloods unremarkable. OGTT normal. Anatomy USS at 20 weeks normal. No urine GBS reported during this pregnancy.

lead_in: At what gestation should the low vaginal-anorectal swab for GBS be collected under a universal screening pathway?

options.text: 28 to 30 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Too early; predictive value for intrapartum colonisation is lower when the swab is taken more than 5 weeks before delivery (RANZCOG; CDC).

options.text: 32 to 34 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Still earlier than the recommended window; the swab is timed to be most predictive of intrapartum colonisation status.

options.text: 35 to 37 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Universal screening swab is recommended at 35-37 weeks (some guidelines now 36-37+6) to predict intrapartum colonisation; results remain valid for approximately 5 weeks (RANZCOG; CDC 2019).

options.text: 38 to 40 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Too late; many women will have delivered or be in established labour before a result is available.

options.text: On admission in established labour only
options.rationale: Describes a risk-factor pathway, not the universal culture-based screening pathway used at this service.

explanation.summary: Under a universal culture-based screening pathway, a combined low vaginal-anorectal swab is collected at 35-37 weeks (CDC now 36-0 to 37-6). Positive swab triggers intrapartum benzylpenicillin prophylaxis. Risk-factor pathways are an alternative used in some Australian services.
key_points: Universal screening swab: 35-37 weeks, low vaginal + anorectal
Result valid approximately 5 weeks
IAP indicated for GBS-positive swab, GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy, previous GBS-affected baby, or risk factors (preterm, ROM >18 h, fever >=38 degC)
First-line IAP: benzylpenicillin 3 g IV then 1.8 g 4-hourly until delivery
explanation.pearls: GBS bacteriuria at any time in pregnancy means treat now AND give intrapartum prophylaxis.

sources.label: CDC: Prevention of perinatal group B streptococcal disease

stem: A 34-year-old woman has her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test (CST) through her general practice. She has had two previous CSTs, both negative for oncogenic HPV. She has had three lifetime sexual partners, is in a monogamous relationship of 6 years, is not pregnant, has no abnormal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no pelvic pain. She received the quadrivalent HPV vaccine series at age 14. The self-collected vaginal swab returns: HPV oncogenic 16/18 detected; reflex liquid-based cytology not performed (per NCSP pathway for 16/18 positive results).

data_table.Examination: Speculum not performed today (self-collected swab pathway).
data_table.Investigations: HPV test: HPV 16/18 detected. Reflex LBC: not performed.

lead_in: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, what is the next step in management?

options.text: Repeat HPV test in 12 months
options.rationale: 12-month repeat HPV is the recommended pathway for oncogenic HPV (not 16/18) with reflex LBC showing negative or low-grade abnormality, not for HPV 16/18 positive results (NCSP).

options.text: Repeat HPV test in 5 years
options.rationale: 5-year recall applies only to women whose HPV test is negative (oncogenic HPV not detected) (NCSP).

options.text: Reflex liquid-based cytology, then act on cytology result
options.rationale: Reflex LBC is performed for oncogenic HPV (not 16/18) positive results to risk-stratify, but not for 16/18 positive results, which go directly to colposcopy regardless of cytology (NCSP).

options.text: Refer for colposcopy
options.rationale: HPV 16/18 positivity confers higher CIN3+ risk; the NCSP directs all HPV 16/18 positive screens to colposcopy regardless of cytology, with reflex LBC performed to inform colposcopic assessment (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Refer for cone biopsy
options.rationale: Cone biopsy is a treatment for histologically confirmed high-grade disease, not a screening-pathway step (NCSP).

explanation.summary: Under the NCSP, HPV 16/18 detected always means colposcopy referral. Other oncogenic HPV types are triaged by reflex LBC. Self-collection has equivalent sensitivity for high-grade disease.
key_points: HPV 16/18 positive: direct colposcopy
Other oncogenic HPV positive: reflex LBC -> if negative/pLSIL/LSIL, 12-month repeat HPV; if HSIL+/possible HSIL, colposcopy
HPV negative: 5-yearly recall (or earlier if symptomatic)
Routine screening: every 5 years from age 25 to 74
explanation.pearls: Self-collection became universally available in July 2022 in Australia and has equivalent sensitivity to clinician-collected samples for CIN2+.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines
Australian Government Department of Health: NCSP

stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her child health nurse appointment 6 weeks after an uncomplicated vaginal birth at term. She describes persistent low mood for the past 4 weeks, poor sleep beyond what is explained by the infant's needs, tearfulness, anhedonia and intrusive thoughts of being a 'bad mother'. She denies thoughts of self-harm or harm to the baby. There is no history of bipolar disorder, prior psychiatric admissions, or postpartum psychosis. She has a supportive partner and breastfeeds without difficulty. There are no language or literacy barriers. The nurse plans to administer a structured screening instrument before involving the GP for further assessment.

data_table.Examination: Affect flat. No psychotic features. Infant thriving on breastfeeds.
data_table.Investigations: TSH normal 4 weeks ago. Hb 116 g/L. No drug or alcohol use.

lead_in: Which screening instrument is recommended in Australian perinatal mental health guidelines for routine postnatal depression screening?

options.text: Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS)
options.rationale: The EPDS is the recommended Australian and international screening tool for antenatal and postnatal depression; a score >=13 indicates probable major depression and item 10 screens for self-harm ideation (COPE guideline 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline 2023

options.text: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9)
options.rationale: PHQ-9 is validated for general depression screening but the perinatal-specific EPDS is preferred in the postnatal period and is the tool nominated by Australian guidance (COPE).

options.text: Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II)
options.rationale: BDI-II is a diagnostic and severity tool, not a routine perinatal screening instrument (COPE).

options.text: Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS)
options.rationale: HADS is used in medically ill populations and is not the recommended perinatal screening tool (COPE).

options.text: Generalised Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-7)
options.rationale: GAD-7 screens for anxiety disorders, not depression; it may complement EPDS but does not replace it (COPE).

explanation.summary: EPDS is the recommended perinatal depression screen in Australia, used at booking, 6-12 weeks postnatal, and as clinically indicated. Item 10 (self-harm) is a flagged safety question requiring same-day follow-up if positive.
key_points: EPDS >=13 = probable major depression; >=10 = possible depression
Always review item 10 (self-harm) regardless of total score
Combine with psychosocial assessment (e.g. ANRQ)
Postpartum psychosis is a different condition requiring emergency psychiatric review
explanation.pearls: Postpartum blues peak at days 3-5 and resolve by 2 weeks; persistent symptoms beyond 2 weeks warrant EPDS screening and clinical assessment.

sources.label: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period
RANZCOG: Mental health care in the perinatal period

subtopic: Menopause MHT - first-line for vasomotor symptoms
stem: A 52-year-old woman attends her general practitioner with 14 months of severe vasomotor symptoms (multiple hot flushes daily, drenching night sweats, sleep disruption). She has had no menstrual period for 18 months. She has an intact uterus, no history of breast cancer, no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism, no migraine with aura, BMI 26, BP 124/78, non-smoker, and minimal alcohol intake. Her FBC, LFTs, lipids and TSH are normal. Mammogram and cervical screening are up to date. She has tried non-pharmacological measures without benefit and would like to commence menopausal hormone therapy. She has read about transdermal oestrogen and asks which regimen is recommended.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable. No breast lumps.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, LFTs, lipids, TSH normal. Mammogram normal. Cervical screening up to date.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line MHT regimen?

options.text: Oral conjugated equine oestrogen alone
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in a woman with an intact uterus risks endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma; progestogen must be added (AMS; RANZCOG).

options.text: Oral oestradiol with cyclical oral medroxyprogesterone
options.rationale: Oral oestrogen has a higher VTE risk than transdermal and is not preferred first-line when transdermal is available; cyclical progestogen is used in perimenopause but this woman is postmenopausal (>12 months amenorrhoea) (AMS).

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch with continuous combined micronised progesterone
options.rationale: Transdermal oestradiol (lower VTE risk than oral) plus continuous micronised progesterone (favourable breast and metabolic profile) is the preferred first-line MHT for a postmenopausal woman with intact uterus (AMS; NICE NG23).

options.text: Tibolone 2.5 mg daily
options.rationale: Tibolone is a reasonable option but not first-line; it has a higher stroke risk in older women and is generally reserved when standard oestrogen-progestogen MHT is unsuitable (AMS).

options.text: Vaginal oestriol cream 0.5 mg twice weekly
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen treats genitourinary symptoms but does not relieve systemic vasomotor symptoms (AMS).

explanation.summary: First-line MHT for systemic vasomotor symptoms in a postmenopausal woman with intact uterus: transdermal oestradiol plus continuous combined micronised progesterone. Transdermal route avoids first-pass effect and reduces VTE/stroke risk; micronised progesterone has a more favourable breast cancer signal than synthetic progestogens.
key_points: Intact uterus = progestogen always
Transdermal oestradiol preferred (lower VTE risk)
Continuous combined regimen once postmenopausal (>12 months amenorrhoea)
Vaginal oestrogen for isolated GSM only
explanation.pearls: Window of opportunity: starting MHT within 10 years of menopause and before age 60 has the most favourable cardiovascular profile.

sources.label: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement
NICE NG23: Menopause diagnosis and management

stem: A 27-year-old G2P0 (one prior tubal ectopic managed by salpingostomy 18 months ago) presents to the early pregnancy assessment unit at an estimated 6 weeks and 4 days gestation by LMP with 36 hours of crampy left lower quadrant pain and intermittent dark vaginal spotting. She is haemodynamically stable. The current pregnancy was conceived spontaneously and she did not use contraception. She has no shoulder tip pain. Her partner has accompanied her.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, focal left iliac fossa tenderness, no peritonism. Speculum: minimal dark blood, os closed. Bimanual: tender left adnexa.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 2,400 IU/L today (1,800 IU/L 48 hours ago). Hb 124 g/L, platelets 240. Blood group A Rh negative, antibody negative. TVUS: empty uterus, no intrauterine gestational sac or pseudosac, 2.1 cm complex left adnexal mass separate from ovary with surrounding ring of vascularity, small amount of free fluid in pouch of Douglas.

lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Threatened miscarriage of intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Beta-hCG is above the discriminatory zone yet there is no intrauterine gestational sac visible on TVUS, making a viable intrauterine pregnancy unlikely (RANZCOG).

options.text: Complete miscarriage with retained corpus luteum
options.rationale: Complete miscarriage typically shows falling beta-hCG, not the suboptimal rise seen here, and would not produce a complex adnexal mass with peripheral vascularity (KEMH).

options.text: Pregnancy of unknown location (PUL) requiring repeat beta-hCG
options.rationale: PUL is a working label only when TVUS shows no intrauterine or extrauterine pregnancy; here a complex adnexal mass with ring of vascularity is identified, which is sonographically diagnostic of ectopic pregnancy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Tubal ectopic pregnancy
options.rationale: Beta-hCG above the discriminatory zone with empty uterus, an adnexal mass separate from the ovary showing the 'ring of fire' peripheral vascularity, free fluid, and a suboptimal beta-hCG trajectory in a woman with previous ectopic is essentially diagnostic of ectopic pregnancy (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Haemorrhagic corpus luteum cyst with concurrent early IUP
options.rationale: A haemorrhagic corpus luteum is intra-ovarian; the described mass is separate from the ovary, and no intrauterine sac is seen despite hCG above the discriminatory zone (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Diagnostic features here: hCG above discriminatory zone (>=1,500-2,000 IU/L) with empty uterus, complex adnexal mass separate from the ovary with peripheral ring of vascularity, free fluid, and a suboptimal hCG rise (<63% in 48 h) in a woman with previous tubal ectopic.
key_points: Discriminatory zone: 1,500-2,000 IU/L
Adnexal mass separate from ovary with ring of fire = ectopic
Suboptimal hCG rise <63% in 48 h is abnormal
Previous ectopic increases recurrence risk 10-20%
Anti-D 250 IU IM if Rh negative in first trimester
explanation.pearls: An empty uterus with hCG above the discriminatory zone is ectopic until proven otherwise. Heterotopic pregnancy is rare in spontaneous conception.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of early pregnancy problems

stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 woman attends for booking ultrasound at an estimated 10 weeks and 2 days gestation by certain LMP. She has had no bleeding or abdominal pain. Her early pregnancy symptoms (nausea, breast tenderness) settled approximately 7 days ago. She has no significant medical history, is on folate 500 mcg and iodine 150 mcg daily, and has no allergies. The transvaginal ultrasound is performed by an accredited sonographer with appropriate views obtained.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Speculum not performed.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 38,000 IU/L. TVUS findings: single gestational sac in uterine fundus, mean sac diameter 26 mm; fetal pole present with crown-rump length 9 mm; no fetal cardiac activity observed after 5 minutes of careful observation by two operators; no subchorionic haematoma.

lead_in: Which finding most definitively confirms early pregnancy loss in this scan?

options.text: Crown-rump length >=7 mm with no fetal cardiac activity on TVUS
options.rationale: Established TVUS criteria for early pregnancy loss are CRL >=7 mm with no fetal cardiac activity, or mean sac diameter >=25 mm with no visible embryo; once an embryo is present the CRL criterion applies (NICE NG126; RANZCOG).

options.text: Mean gestational sac diameter >=25 mm with no embryo
options.rationale: A valid criterion but does not apply here because an embryo is clearly visible with measurable CRL (NICE NG126).

options.text: Beta-hCG plateau over 48 hours
options.rationale: Suggestive of non-viable pregnancy but never diagnostic alone; TVUS criteria are required to confirm loss (RANZCOG).

options.text: Loss of pregnancy symptoms in the first trimester
options.rationale: Non-specific clinical sign that does not establish the diagnosis (RANZCOG).

options.text: Mean sac diameter to CRL ratio less than 5 mm
options.rationale: Small sac size relative to CRL is a marker of higher risk of loss but is not a definitive diagnostic criterion in current guidelines (NICE NG126).

explanation.summary: Strict TVUS criteria are used to diagnose early pregnancy loss because of the consequences of acting on a viable pregnancy. CRL >=7 mm with absent cardiac activity, or MSD >=25 mm with no embryo, are the two main criteria. Borderline measurements warrant repeat scan in 7-14 days.
key_points: CRL >=7 mm + no fetal heart = early pregnancy loss
MSD >=25 mm + no embryo = early pregnancy loss
If uncertain, repeat in 7-14 days (or use a second operator)
Management options: expectant, medical (misoprostol +/- mifepristone), surgical
explanation.pearls: When in doubt, wait and repeat. Acting on borderline measurements risks terminating a viable pregnancy.

sources.label: NICE NG126: Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 36 weeks and 4 days gestation is brought to the birth suite by ambulance following a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting 90 seconds. She had been complaining of a 24-hour frontal headache and epigastric pain. There is no known history of epilepsy, head injury, illicit drug use or hyponatraemia. She had documented systolic readings of 150-160 mmHg at her two most recent antenatal visits with proteinuria 2+ on dipstick. On arrival she is post-ictal, drowsy but maintaining her airway, and there is no further seizure activity. The fetal heart rate is 138 bpm with a reassuring CTG.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/108 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 96% RA, T 37.0.
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, GCS 13, brisk reflexes with sustained clonus. Abdomen soft, fundus 36 cm, cephalic presentation.
data_table.Investigations: Urine protein:creatinine 145 mg/mmol. Hb 118, platelets 92, AST 88, ALT 76, urate 0.42, creatinine 96, LDH 480. CTG: baseline 140, variability 10, accelerations present, no decelerations.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line drug to administer now to prevent further seizures?

options.text: IV diazepam 5 mg
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines may be used to terminate an active prolonged seizure but are not first-line for seizure prevention in eclampsia and carry neonatal respiratory depression risk (SOMANZ; Cochrane).

options.text: IV phenytoin loading 15 mg/kg
options.rationale: Phenytoin is inferior to magnesium sulfate for both treatment and prevention of eclamptic seizures (Cochrane Eclampsia Trial).

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g/hour maintenance
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is the agent of choice for eclampsia and severe pre-eclampsia, reducing recurrent seizures by approximately 50% versus diazepam or phenytoin; SOMANZ and RANZCOG recommend 4 g IV over 5-20 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours (SOMANZ 2023; Cochrane).

options.text: IV levetiracetam 1.5 g
options.rationale: Levetiracetam is not a recommended first-line agent in eclampsia; magnesium sulfate has the strongest evidence (SOMANZ).

options.text: Immediate Caesarean section under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Delivery is required after maternal stabilisation but is not the immediate anticonvulsant; stabilise mother first with magnesium, then control BP, then plan birth (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Magnesium sulfate is first-line for prevention and treatment of eclamptic seizures. Typical regimen: 4 g IV over 5-20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour for 24 hours, with monitoring for toxicity (reflexes, respiratory rate, urine output).
key_points: MgSO4 reduces recurrent seizures by ~50% vs diazepam/phenytoin (Cochrane)
Monitor: patellar reflexes, RR (>=12), UO (>=100 mL/4 h), SpO2
Toxicity antidote: 10% calcium gluconate 10 mL IV slow
Antihypertensive (labetalol, hydralazine or nifedipine) for systolic >=160 or diastolic >=110
Plan birth after maternal stabilisation
explanation.pearls: Stabilise then deliver. Birth is the only definitive treatment but premature delivery before maternal stabilisation worsens outcomes.

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 woman at 31 weeks and 2 days gestation presents with sudden painless bright red vaginal bleeding while resting on the couch. The bleeding has filled one sanitary pad in 30 minutes. She has had no abdominal pain, no contractions, no rupture of membranes and reports normal fetal movements. Her previous two births were by lower-segment Caesarean section for breech and for failure to progress. At her 20-week morphology scan the placenta was reported as anterior, fundal and well clear of the internal os. She is a non-smoker, denies cocaine or other substance use, and there has been no recent intercourse or trauma. She is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.7, SpO2 98% RA.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender, uterus relaxed, fundal height 32 cm. Cephalic presentation. Speculum (no digital VE): moderate fresh blood pooling in vagina, cervix appears closed.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 116, platelets 240, coags normal. Group A Rh positive. CTG reactive. Bedside USS now: posterior placenta with leading edge 8 mm from internal os.

lead_in: What is the most likely cause of her antepartum haemorrhage?

options.text: Placental abruption
options.rationale: Abruption typically presents with painful bleeding, tonically contracted uterus, and possible fetal compromise; this woman has painless bleeding with a soft non-tender uterus and reassuring CTG (RANZCOG APH).

options.text: Low-lying placenta / placenta praevia
options.rationale: Painless bright red bleeding in the late second or third trimester with a placental edge within 20 mm of the internal os on USS defines a low-lying placenta and is the most likely cause here (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Vasa praevia
options.rationale: Vasa praevia bleeds at the time of membrane rupture and is typically associated with rapid fetal compromise (sinusoidal CTG, bradycardia); membranes here are intact and CTG is reassuring (RANZCOG).

options.text: Uterine rupture along a prior LSCS scar
options.rationale: Rupture typically presents with severe abdominal pain, loss of station, fetal bradycardia and maternal compromise during labour; this patient is not labouring and is stable (RANZCOG).

options.text: Cervical ectropion bleed
options.rationale: Cervical lesions cause minor postcoital spotting; volume of bleeding here (full pad in 30 min) is excessive for an ectropion and pooling of fresh blood with a closed cervix points elsewhere (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Painless bright red bleeding after 20 weeks with a low-lying placenta on USS is placenta praevia (low-lying placenta in updated nomenclature: placental edge <20 mm from internal os). Avoid digital vaginal examination until praevia excluded.
key_points: Updated terminology (RANZCOG 2018): low-lying placenta if edge within 20 mm of internal os; placenta praevia if covering os
Painless bleeding + soft uterus + abnormal placental location = praevia
Confirm with TVUS (safe)
Anti-D if Rh negative; admit, IV access, group and hold, CTG, plan steroids if <34/40 and considering preterm birth
20-week scan may relocate as the lower segment forms - repeat at 32 weeks if low-lying
explanation.pearls: Painful + tender uterus = think abruption. Painless + soft uterus = think praevia. ROM-triggered fetal bradycardia = think vasa praevia.

sources.label: KEMH: Placenta praevia and accreta spectrum

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 38 weeks and 6 days gestation is admitted in early labour. The fetus is in transverse lie despite an ECV attempt one week prior, and the obstetric team is considering Caesarean section. Spontaneous rupture of membranes occurs during the admission examination with a moderate gush of clear liquor. Immediately afterward the midwife identifies a pulsating loop of umbilical cord protruding through the cervix into the vagina. The CTG shows an abrupt fetal heart rate deceleration to 80 bpm. The obstetric team is being called and the emergency theatre is being readied for Category 1 Caesarean section.

data_table.Examination: Cord palpable in vagina, cervix 3 cm dilated, transverse lie, fetal back inferior. Membranes ruptured (clear liquor).
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged fetal bradycardia 80 bpm. FBC, group and hold sent.

lead_in: What is the most important immediate manoeuvre while the theatre is being prepared?

options.text: Replace the cord into the uterus
options.rationale: Cord replacement is not recommended; it may cause vasospasm or compression. Hands-off the cord, or minimal handling only if needed to elevate the presenting part (RANZCOG; PROMPT).

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord and place the mother in knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position
options.rationale: Manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord (vaginal hand pushing the presenting part upwards) and maternal positioning (knee-chest or Trendelenburg) until delivery is the immediate action to relieve cord compression while preparing for Category 1 CS (RANZCOG; PROMPT).

options.text: Start oxytocin augmentation to expedite vaginal birth
options.rationale: Augmentation will worsen cord compression and is contraindicated; this patient also has a transverse lie and is at 3 cm, so vaginal birth is not feasible (RANZCOG).

options.text: Fill the bladder with 500 mL saline via Foley catheter only
options.rationale: Bladder filling can elevate the presenting part and is a useful adjunct if transfer is prolonged but is not the most immediate manoeuvre; manual elevation and maternal positioning come first (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 mcg SC to reduce contractions
options.rationale: Acute tocolysis may be considered as an adjunct if uterine activity is impairing perfusion or transfer is delayed, but the immediate priority is manual elevation and positioning (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency. Immediate priorities: call for help, manual elevation of presenting part off the cord, maternal positioning (knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg), Category 1 Caesarean section if not at full dilatation. Bladder filling and tocolysis are adjuncts if transfer is delayed.
key_points: Risk factors: malpresentation, polyhydramnios, prematurity, ARM with high station
Minimal handling of the cord; keep moist and warm
Cat 1 CS unless full dilatation with imminent vaginal birth
Continuous CTG until delivery
Document call-to-delivery interval; target <30 minutes
explanation.pearls: Knee-chest position uses gravity to displace the presenting part off the cord and is especially useful during transfer.

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman attends the birth suite at 39 weeks and 1 day gestation reporting a sudden gush of clear fluid 4 hours ago with ongoing leakage. She has no contractions, no fever, normal fetal movements, and no per-vaginal bleeding. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated: booking bloods normal, OGTT normal at 28 weeks, group B streptococcus low vaginal-anorectal swab at 36 weeks was negative, and her 20-week anatomy scan was normal. She has no allergies, takes no regular medications, and is a non-smoker.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/70 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.7, RR 16.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, fundus 39 cm, cephalic, longitudinal lie. Sterile speculum: clear fluid pooling in posterior fornix, cervix appears favourable, no obvious cord. No digital VE performed.
data_table.Investigations: Maternal observations unremarkable. CTG reactive, baseline 140, variability 12, accelerations present, no decelerations. Bedside USS: AFI 9 cm, cephalic, normal Doppler.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Discharge home with daily review and induction if not in labour by 96 hours
options.rationale: Expectant management for that long at term exceeds Australian guidance; chorioamnionitis risk rises beyond ~24 hours (RANZCOG).

options.text: Offer induction of labour now or expectant management for up to 24 hours, with shared decision-making
options.rationale: At term with confirmed PROM, RANZCOG recommends offering either immediate induction or expectant management for up to 24 hours, with informed consent; immediate induction reduces chorioamnionitis risk slightly without increasing CS rates (RANZCOG; TERMPROM trial).

options.text: Immediate Caesarean section regardless of patient preference
options.rationale: PROM at term is not an indication for CS; vaginal birth is appropriate where there is no other contraindication (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics now regardless of GBS status or temperature
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not indicated in afebrile term PROM with negative GBS; reserve antibiotics for clinical chorioamnionitis or GBS-positive status (RANZCOG).

options.text: Tocolysis with nifedipine and corticosteroids for fetal lung maturation
options.rationale: Tocolysis and steroids are not indicated at 39+1 weeks; both are for preterm gestations (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Term PROM: offer induction of labour (oxytocin or prostaglandin if unfavourable cervix) or expectant management up to 24 hours with informed consent. The TERMPROM trial showed lower chorioamnionitis and neonatal infection with immediate induction; CS rates were similar.
key_points: Confirm PROM by sterile speculum (pooling) - avoid digital VE
Continuous CTG; assess for chorioamnionitis (maternal fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, fetal tachycardia, malodorous discharge)
Group B Streptococcus status guides intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis
Anti-D not required at term gestation for PROM alone
explanation.pearls: Document the time of membrane rupture - the 18-hour threshold for IAP in some GBS pathways starts from rupture, not from labour.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes

stem: A 24-year-old nulligravid woman attends her general practice with a 3-year history of progressively worsening cyclical pelvic pain. Pain begins 1-2 days before menses, peaks on day 1-2, and is associated with deep dyspareunia and occasional dyschezia. She has no urinary symptoms, no postcoital bleeding and a normal cervical screening test 18 months ago. She has tried regular ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily during menses with partial benefit. She is in a stable monogamous relationship, is sexually active and is not currently planning pregnancy in the next 2 years; she is open to hormonal therapy and has no contraindications to oestrogen.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Bimanual: uterus anteverted, mobile, mildly tender, no adnexal masses, no palpable nodularity in the pouch of Douglas (this can be normal in early endometriosis).
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic USS: normal ovaries, no endometriomas, no deep infiltrating lesions identified.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy before initiating therapy
options.rationale: Empirical hormonal therapy is recommended before laparoscopy; surgery is offered if empirical treatment fails or imaging suggests deep disease or endometrioma (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Commence GnRH agonist (e.g. goserelin) with add-back therapy
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are second-line after failure of COCP or progestogens due to cost, side effects and bone density concerns (ESHRE).

options.text: Trial of continuous combined oral contraceptive pill plus NSAID as needed
options.rationale: First-line empirical therapy for suspected endometriosis without red flags is hormonal suppression with COCP (continuous preferable for symptom control) or progestogen, plus NSAID for breakthrough pain; effective in 70-80% (RANZCOG; ESHRE 2022).

options.text: Refer for hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy
options.rationale: Definitive surgery is inappropriate in a 24-year-old who has not completed her family and has not trialled medical therapy (RANZCOG).

options.text: CA-125 testing to confirm diagnosis
options.rationale: CA-125 has poor sensitivity and specificity for endometriosis and is not recommended as a diagnostic test (ESHRE).

explanation.summary: Empirical hormonal suppression (COCP, progestogens or LNG-IUS) plus NSAIDs is first-line for suspected endometriosis. Imaging may be normal in superficial peritoneal disease; absence of findings on USS does not exclude endometriosis. Laparoscopy is reserved for failed medical therapy, suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis or fertility concerns.
key_points: Empirical hormonal therapy before laparoscopy
Continuous COCP reduces menstruation and pain
LNG-IUS, dienogest 2 mg daily and DMPA are alternatives
Pelvic USS may be normal in superficial endometriosis
Refer to gynaecologist if no response in 3-6 months or red flags
explanation.pearls: Dyspareunia + dyschezia + cyclical pelvic pain + relief with menses ending is the classical history; consider endometriosis even with a normal pelvic ultrasound.

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP with 18 months of oligomenorrhoea (cycles 45-90 days), facial and chest hair growth requiring waxing every 3 weeks, and weight gain of 6 kg over 12 months. She is not sexually active currently and not using contraception. She is not seeking fertility. There is no galactorrhoea, no temperature intolerance, no headaches and no visual symptoms. Her BMI is 29. The clinician undertakes appropriate investigations to assess for polycystic ovary syndrome and other causes of androgen excess.

data_table.Examination: BMI 29. Modified Ferriman-Gallwey score 12 (hirsutism). Acanthosis nigricans at nape. No virilisation. No goitre.
data_table.Investigations: Total testosterone mildly elevated, SHBG low, free androgen index elevated. 17-OHP normal. TSH normal. Prolactin normal. Beta-hCG negative. Day-of-test LH:FSH 2.5. Pelvic USS: each ovary contains 22 follicles 2-9 mm, ovarian volume 11 mL each. Fasting glucose 5.4, HbA1c 5.7%.

lead_in: Using the 2023 International Evidence-Based PCOS Guideline (Rotterdam-derived criteria), how many of the following are required to make the diagnosis: (i) oligo-anovulation, (ii) clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, (iii) polycystic ovaries on USS or elevated AMH?

options.text: All three features are required
options.rationale: Rotterdam (and the 2023 international PCOS guideline) require any 2 of 3 features, not all 3 (Teede et al. 2023).

options.text: Any two of the three features are required
options.rationale: Rotterdam criteria (endorsed and updated by the 2023 international evidence-based PCOS guideline) require 2 of 3: oligo-anovulation, hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical), and polycystic ovarian morphology on USS or elevated AMH, after exclusion of other causes (Teede et al. 2023).

options.text: Hyperandrogenism alone is sufficient
options.rationale: That describes the older androgen-excess-PCOS Society criteria modified, not Rotterdam (Teede et al. 2023).

options.text: Polycystic ovaries on USS alone is sufficient
options.rationale: Polycystic morphology occurs in 20-30% of asymptomatic young women and is not diagnostic alone (Teede et al. 2023).

options.text: Elevated LH:FSH ratio is required
options.rationale: LH:FSH ratio is no longer part of any diagnostic criteria for PCOS (Teede et al. 2023).

explanation.summary: PCOS diagnosis (Rotterdam, endorsed by 2023 international guideline): 2 of 3 of oligo-anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology (>=20 follicles per ovary on high-resolution TVUS or ovarian volume >=10 mL, or elevated AMH), after exclusion of other causes (thyroid, hyperprolactinaemia, non-classical CAH, Cushing's, androgen-secreting tumour).
key_points: Rotterdam: 2/3 features
Exclude mimics: TFTs, prolactin, 17-OHP +/- DHEAS
Within 8 years of menarche: hyperandrogenism + oligo-anovulation (USS unreliable)
Long-term: screen for IGT/T2DM, dyslipidaemia, endometrial protection, mental health
Lifestyle is first-line; metformin and COCP common adjuncts
explanation.pearls: USS appearance of polycystic ovaries alone, without clinical or biochemical features, does not make PCOS.

sources.label: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

stem: A 44-year-old G3P3 woman attends her general practitioner with 8 months of progressively heavier menstrual bleeding. Her cycles remain regular at 28 days but periods now last 9-10 days with passage of clots and she requires double protection (tampon and pad) on the heaviest days. She has needed to take time off work and has felt increasingly fatigued. There is no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding. She is not on hormonal therapy or anticoagulants. There is no family history of bleeding disorders. Cervical screening is up to date and negative. She had a normal pelvic ultrasound 18 months ago for unrelated symptoms.

data_table.Examination: BMI 26. Speculum: cervix appears normal. Bimanual: anteverted uterus, irregularly enlarged to approximately 12-week size, non-tender.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L (microcytic), ferritin 9 ng/mL, TSH 1.6, beta-hCG negative, coagulation profile normal, von Willebrand screen normal. TVUS today: uterus 11 x 7 x 6 cm with two intramural fibroids (4.5 cm and 3.2 cm) distorting the cavity; endometrial thickness 9 mm and homogeneous; ovaries normal.

lead_in: Using the FIGO PALM-COEIN classification, which is the most likely cause of her abnormal uterine bleeding?

options.text: AUB-P (polyp)
options.rationale: No endometrial polyp is seen on TVUS; endometrium is homogeneous (FIGO PALM-COEIN).

options.text: AUB-A (adenomyosis)
options.rationale: USS features of adenomyosis (globular uterus, asymmetric myometrium, sub-endometrial linear striations) are not described; intramural fibroids are the dominant finding (FIGO).

options.text: AUB-L (leiomyoma)
options.rationale: Two intramural fibroids distorting the cavity (FIGO type 2-5 depending on location) most plausibly explain heavy regular menses and an enlarged irregular uterus; classification is AUB-L (FIGO PALM-COEIN).

options.text: AUB-O (ovulatory dysfunction)
options.rationale: Ovulatory dysfunction is typically associated with irregular cycles; her cycles remain regular at 28 days (FIGO).

options.text: AUB-C (coagulopathy)
options.rationale: Coagulation studies and von Willebrand screen are normal, and there is no personal or family bleeding history (FIGO).

explanation.summary: FIGO PALM-COEIN classifies AUB into structural (Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia) and non-structural (Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified). Heavy regular menses + enlarged irregular uterus + intramural fibroids on USS = AUB-L.
key_points: PALM = structural, COEIN = non-structural
Leiomyomas sub-classified 0-8 by location (FIGO)
Investigations for HMB: FBC, ferritin, TFTs, beta-hCG, TVUS
Endometrial sampling if >=45 years or risk factors (obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, persistent IMB)
Treat iron deficiency anaemia in parallel
explanation.pearls: Persistent intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding always needs further investigation regardless of imaging (consider hysteroscopy).

stem: A 36-year-old G3P1 woman (one term vaginal birth, one first-trimester miscarriage) is reviewed at 33 weeks and 4 days gestation in the maternal-fetal medicine clinic. Her booking BP was 122/76 mmHg. She has chronic hypertension diagnosed at 28 years of age, well-controlled on labetalol 200 mg twice daily through pregnancy. Booking bloods showed Hb 124, platelets 235, urate 0.28, creatinine 68, AST 22, ALT 18, normal LFTs. She had a first-trimester combined risk screen and a normal 20-week morphology scan. She received aspirin 150 mg nocte from 12 weeks based on her chronic hypertension and age. She has had no headache, no visual symptoms, no epigastric pain and reports normal fetal movements. She is a non-smoker, BMI 31, allergic to penicillin (rash), influenza-vaccinated this pregnancy and has had her pertussis booster at 28 weeks. Family history: mother had pre-eclampsia and sister had HELLP at 32 weeks. Today she reports mild ankle swelling and the midwife notes 1+ proteinuria on dipstick.

data_table.Vital signs: BP today 152/96 mmHg (repeated, confirmed). HR 84. T 36.7.
data_table.Examination: Fundus 33 cm, cephalic, fetal movements palpable. Brisk reflexes, no clonus. Mild bipedal oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Spot urine protein:creatinine ratio 38 mg/mmol. Hb 122, platelets 188 (was 235), creatinine 78 (was 68), urate 0.36, AST 28, ALT 24, LDH 240, normal coags. Fetal growth USS today: EFW 30th centile (was 45th at 28 weeks), umbilical artery PI 95th centile, normal MCA, AFI 9. CTG reactive.

lead_in: Using SOMANZ 2023 criteria, what is the most accurate diagnosis?

options.text: Worsening chronic hypertension only
options.rationale: Although BP has risen, additional features (new proteinuria with PCR >=30 mg/mmol, falling platelets, rising creatinine, rising urate, falling fetal growth centile, abnormal umbilical artery Doppler) meet SOMANZ criteria for superimposed pre-eclampsia (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: Gestational hypertension
options.rationale: She has pre-existing chronic hypertension; the term is reserved for new hypertension after 20 weeks without features of pre-eclampsia (SOMANZ).

options.text: Pre-eclampsia superimposed on chronic hypertension
options.rationale: Chronic hypertension with new-onset proteinuria (PCR 38 mg/mmol, threshold >=30) plus haematological involvement (platelets fallen by ~20%), renal involvement (creatinine risen) and uteroplacental dysfunction (falling growth centile, abnormal UA Doppler) meets SOMANZ 2023 criteria for superimposed pre-eclampsia (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).

options.text: HELLP syndrome
options.rationale: HELLP requires haemolysis (low haptoglobin, schistocytes, LDH >600), elevated transaminases (typically >70) and platelets <100; she has not met those thresholds (SOMANZ).

options.text: Isolated fetal growth restriction
options.rationale: FGR is part of the picture but the maternal blood pressure, proteinuria and end-organ changes mean the syndrome is pre-eclampsia with FGR as a manifestation (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: SOMANZ 2023 defines pre-eclampsia as hypertension after 20 weeks plus any new maternal or uteroplacental organ involvement (renal, hepatic, haematological, neurological, pulmonary oedema, or uteroplacental dysfunction with FGR/abnormal Doppler/stillbirth). In chronic hypertension, the same features superimposed make it 'superimposed pre-eclampsia'.
key_points: Proteinuria threshold: PCR >=30 mg/mmol or >=300 mg/24 h or >=2+ dipstick after exclusion of UTI
Severe features: BP >=160/110, platelets <100, creatinine >=90, AST/ALT >2x normal, pulmonary oedema, neurological signs, severe FGR with abnormal Doppler
Aspirin 100-150 mg nocte from 12-16 weeks reduces pre-eclampsia in high-risk women
Birth is curative; balance maternal risk against gestation and fetal maturity
Magnesium for severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia
explanation.pearls: Proteinuria is not required to diagnose pre-eclampsia under SOMANZ if other maternal or uteroplacental organ dysfunction is present.

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman (one prior uncomplicated term vaginal birth 3 years ago, birth weight 3,650 g) attends for her routine 28-week 75 g oral glucose tolerance test. She has a BMI of 30 (pre-pregnancy 28), a sister with type 2 diabetes diagnosed at age 38, and identifies as South Asian. Her booking HbA1c was 5.5% and booking fasting glucose was 4.7 mmol/L. The pregnancy has been otherwise uncomplicated: BP averaging 110-118/68-74, fundal height appropriate, fetal movements normal, 20-week morphology scan normal. She is on pregnancy multivitamin only, no allergies, non-smoker, social drinker (ceased), and has had her influenza and pertussis vaccinations. She follows a primarily vegetarian diet and walks 30 minutes most days.

data_table.Examination: Fundus 28 cm, cephalic. No glycosuria.
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT today (after 8-hour fast): fasting venous plasma glucose 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour 9.4 mmol/L, 2-hour 8.7 mmol/L. Booking HbA1c 5.5% (37 mmol/mol). Urine ACR normal. TSH 1.4.

lead_in: Using ADIPS 2014 (RANZCOG-endorsed) 75 g OGTT thresholds, which interpretation is correct?

options.text: Normal OGTT; reassure and continue routine care
options.rationale: Fasting 5.3 (>=5.1) and 2-hour 8.7 (>=8.5) both meet ADIPS GDM thresholds; this is not a normal result (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Impaired fasting glucose only - repeat in 4 weeks
options.rationale: ADIPS does not use 'impaired fasting glucose' for GDM diagnosis; any one threshold breach (fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5) diagnoses GDM (ADIPS).

options.text: GDM diagnosed - fasting and 2-hour values both at or above ADIPS thresholds
options.rationale: ADIPS 2014 thresholds: fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L; meeting any single threshold diagnoses GDM. Her fasting (5.3) and 2-hour (8.7) both meet criteria (ADIPS; RANZCOG GDM).

options.text: Overt diabetes in pregnancy - commence insulin immediately
options.rationale: Overt diabetes requires fasting >=7.0, random >=11.1 or HbA1c >=6.5%; her values are below these thresholds (ADIPS).

options.text: GDM diagnosed - 1-hour value alone is diagnostic
options.rationale: Her 1-hour value (9.4) is below the 10.0 threshold; diagnosis here is based on fasting and 2-hour, not 1-hour (ADIPS).

explanation.summary: ADIPS 2014 (RANZCOG-endorsed) 75 g OGTT diagnostic thresholds: fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L. Any one value meeting threshold diagnoses GDM. Overt diabetes thresholds: fasting >=7.0, random >=11.1, HbA1c >=6.5%.
key_points: Universal screening at 24-28 weeks (earlier if risk factors)
GDM management: lifestyle, BGL monitoring, metformin and/or insulin
Targets: fasting <5.0-5.3, 1-h <7.4, 2-h <6.7 (varies slightly by service)
Postpartum 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks to detect persistent T2DM
Lifelong T2DM screening (yearly fasting glucose or HbA1c)
explanation.pearls: ADIPS thresholds (5.1/10.0/8.5) differ from older NDDG and Carpenter-Coustan criteria - know the Australian numbers cold for the exam.

sources.label: ADIPS: Consensus guidelines for diagnosis of GDM 2014

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman is in second-stage labour at 40 weeks and 4 days gestation in a public hospital birth suite. Her two previous births were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries at 3,750 g and 3,920 g. This pregnancy was complicated by gestational diabetes managed on metformin with reasonable glycaemic control (most fasting BGLs 5.0-5.4, postprandial 6.5-7.2). The 36-week growth scan suggested an EFW of 4,250 g (95th centile). She has been pushing for 25 minutes. The fetal head delivers in OA position but appears to retract tightly against the perineum (turtle sign). Routine downward traction does not deliver the anterior shoulder. The midwife calls the obstetric emergency.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 132/82, HR 96, T 36.8. Fetal HR was reassuring prior to delivery of head.
data_table.Examination: Turtle sign positive. Head delivered, no restitution, anterior shoulder impacted behind symphysis. No cord around neck.
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab at 36 weeks negative. Hb 118. CTG: prior to delivery, normal baseline.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line manoeuvre after calling for help?

options.text: Apply fundal pressure to dislodge the anterior shoulder
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated; it worsens impaction and may cause uterine rupture or brachial plexus injury (RANZCOG; PROMPT).

options.text: Perform McRoberts manoeuvre and apply suprapubic pressure
options.rationale: First-line manoeuvre in the HELPERR sequence after calling for help is McRoberts (hyperflexion of maternal hips on abdomen) combined with suprapubic pressure, resolving up to 50-70% of cases (RANZCOG; PROMPT; ACOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to Zavanelli manoeuvre and Caesarean section
options.rationale: Zavanelli is a last-resort procedure attempted after exhaustion of all other manoeuvres; it is not first-line (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform symphysiotomy
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is rarely used in high-resource settings and only as a last resort with appropriate skills (RANZCOG).

options.text: Cut the umbilical cord and proceed to Caesarean
options.rationale: The cord cannot be cut while the fetus is still impacted; this would cause severe hypoxia. Shoulder dystocia is managed vaginally with the HELPERR sequence (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia management uses the HELPERR mnemonic: Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter (internal rotation: Rubin II, Wood's screw), Remove posterior arm, Roll (Gaskin all-fours). McRoberts and suprapubic pressure together resolve most cases.
key_points: Call for help: obstetrician, anaesthetist, paediatrician, additional midwife, scribe
Avoid fundal pressure (worsens impaction)
Avoid excessive head traction (brachial plexus injury)
Document time intervals, manoeuvres, fetal position, staff present
After delivery: check for PPH, perineal trauma, neonatal injury (Erb's palsy, fractures, hypoxia)
explanation.pearls: Macrosomia, GDM, prior shoulder dystocia, prolonged second stage and operative vaginal birth are risk factors but most shoulder dystocia is unpredictable.

subtopic: Preterm labour - corticosteroid and tocolysis decision
stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman presents to a tertiary maternity service at 30 weeks and 3 days gestation with 4 hours of regular painful contractions every 4 minutes. Her previous pregnancy ended in a spontaneous vaginal birth at 35 weeks. This pregnancy has been uncomplicated to date: booking bloods normal, OGTT normal, 20-week scan normal, GBS swab not yet done. She is on no regular medications, has no allergies, smoking 5 cigarettes per day (declined to cease), and lives 90 minutes from the nearest tertiary NICU (currently in that NICU). Sterile speculum shows a long closed cervix at booking 4 weeks ago, but on TVUS today her cervical length is 18 mm and fetal fibronectin is positive. Membranes are intact. The fetal heart is reassuring.

data_table.Examination: Contractions palpable every 4 min, moderate intensity. Sterile speculum: long-looking cervix, no liquor pooling, no bleeding. No digital VE performed.
data_table.Investigations: TVUS cervical length 18 mm. Fetal fibronectin positive. Hb 120, WCC 11.8, CRP 6. CTG reactive.

lead_in: Which combination of interventions is most appropriate now?

options.text: Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM stat and again in 24 hours; no tocolysis; magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection if delivery imminent
options.rationale: Tocolysis is indicated to gain 48 hours for steroid effect at this gestation with high-risk features (cervical length <25 mm, positive fFN, regular contractions) (RANZCOG; ACOG).

options.text: Betamethasone, nifedipine for tocolysis, magnesium sulfate for fetal neuroprotection, paediatric consult
options.rationale: At 30 weeks with imminent preterm birth, give antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x 2 doses 24 hours apart), tocolysis with nifedipine (loading then maintenance) to gain time for steroids, and magnesium sulfate for fetal neuroprotection (<30 weeks per some guidelines, <32 weeks per others), with paediatric consult (RANZCOG; ANZCTG; ACOG).

options.text: Atosiban tocolysis alone; corticosteroids only if delivery delayed by more than 24 hours
options.rationale: Corticosteroids should be administered immediately on presentation with threatened preterm labour at this gestation; delaying steroids loses their benefit if birth occurs sooner than expected (RANZCOG).

options.text: Immediate Caesarean section for fetal protection
options.rationale: There is no fetal indication for delivery and her contractions may be amenable to tocolysis; preterm birth should be vaginal unless other obstetric indication (RANZCOG).

options.text: Transfer to a peripheral hospital for ongoing observation
options.rationale: She is already in a tertiary centre with appropriate NICU - in-utero transfer to a lower-level centre is inappropriate (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Threatened preterm labour at 30 weeks with cervical length <25 mm and positive fFN: give antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x 2 doses 24 h apart), tocolysis with nifedipine to gain 48 hours for steroid effect, magnesium sulfate for fetal neuroprotection, and arrange paediatric consult.
key_points: Steroids: most benefit if birth between 24-34+6 weeks; benefits within 24 h to 7 days
Tocolysis: nifedipine first-line; atosiban alternative; contraindications include fetal compromise, chorioamnionitis, severe PE
MgSO4 neuroprotection: usually <30-32 weeks, single 4 g loading + 1 g/h or single dose
GBS prophylaxis indicated in preterm labour without GBS result
Consider in-utero transfer if NICU level inadequate
explanation.pearls: Tocolysis is to gain time for steroids and transfer - it does not improve neonatal outcomes by itself.

sources.label: ANZ Antenatal magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection guideline

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman attends the sexual health service with 5 days of progressive lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia and abnormal vaginal discharge. She is sexually active with a new male partner over the past 2 months, has not used condoms consistently, and is on the COCP. Her cervical screening test is up to date and negative. She has no past surgical history, no allergies and is otherwise well. There is no urinary frequency, dysuria, fever, vomiting or rectal bleeding. She is haemodynamically stable and not pregnant. She has no IUD and no history of ectopic pregnancy. She has not yet had any antibiotics.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen: bilateral lower quadrant tenderness, no peritonism, no mass. Speculum: mucopurulent cervical discharge, friable cervix. Bimanual: marked cervical motion tenderness, bilateral adnexal tenderness, no palpable mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Self-collected vaginal swab NAAT for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, Mycoplasma genitalium pending. HIV/syphilis/HBV/HCV sent. WCC 13.2, CRP 42. TVUS: no tubo-ovarian abscess, free fluid in pouch of Douglas. Urinalysis negative.

lead_in: Which empirical outpatient antibiotic regimen is recommended by Therapeutic Guidelines for mild-moderate PID?

options.text: Amoxicillin 1 g orally three times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin alone does not cover gonorrhoea, chlamydia or anaerobes and is inadequate for PID (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Quinolone resistance in Neisseria gonorrhoeae is high in Australia; ciprofloxacin monotherapy is not recommended (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy
options.rationale: Doxycycline alone misses gonorrhoea (requires ceftriaxone) and anaerobes (requires metronidazole) (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose, plus doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days, plus metronidazole 400 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines recommends combined cover of gonorrhoea (ceftriaxone), chlamydia/Mycoplasma genitalium (doxycycline), and anaerobes (metronidazole) for outpatient PID; treat empirically before NAAT results (TG; AusSTI).

options.text: Azithromycin 1 g orally single dose only
options.rationale: Single-dose azithromycin is insufficient for PID and increases macrolide resistance in Mycoplasma genitalium; combination 14-day therapy is required (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Outpatient PID (mild-moderate): ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat + doxycycline 100 mg PO BD for 14 days + metronidazole 400 mg PO BD for 14 days. Inpatient (severe, tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, IUD with PID, failed outpatient): IV ceftriaxone + IV/PO doxycycline + IV/PO metronidazole, with surgical drainage for TOA.
key_points: Treat empirically; do not wait for NAAT
Trace and treat sexual partners
Test for HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B/C
Mycoplasma genitalium increasingly implicated; consider moxifloxacin if M. gen confirmed and doxycycline failure
Counsel about increased risk of ectopic, infertility, chronic pelvic pain
explanation.pearls: PID diagnosis is primarily clinical: pelvic/lower abdominal pain plus cervical motion, uterine, or adnexal tenderness in a sexually active woman.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Pelvic inflammatory disease

stem: A 34-year-old G4P3 woman has just delivered a 4,100 g live male infant by spontaneous vaginal birth at 40 weeks and 4 days gestation. Her three previous births were uncomplicated. The third stage was actively managed with oxytocin 10 IU IM. The placenta delivered intact at 8 minutes with no obvious cotyledon defect. Within 5 minutes of placental delivery she has lost an estimated 1,200 mL of fresh red blood with ongoing trickling. On examination the uterus is soft and poorly contracted. There is no obvious genital tract trauma on inspection. She has no known coagulopathy, no anticoagulants, no prior PPH, and was Hb 116 g/L on admission. IV access is in place with 2 large-bore cannulae, fluids are running, group and hold is sent and the obstetric emergency team has been activated. Bimanual compression has been commenced.

data_table.Examination: Uterus soft, fundus at umbilicus, ongoing fresh PV bleed. Perineum: small first-degree tear, not bleeding. No clots in vagina.
data_table.Investigations: Group A Rh positive, antibody screen negative. Coags and FBC sent. Catheter inserted: 250 mL clear urine.

lead_in: Following first-line IV oxytocin bolus and infusion, which is the next-line uterotonic to administer per Australian PPH guidelines?

options.text: IM/IV ergometrine 250 mcg
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension and should be used cautiously; the typical next agent in the Australian PPH stepwise escalation after additional oxytocin is ergometrine where not contraindicated, but in many AU protocols (NSW, KEMH) the next pharmacological step is IM carboprost or sublingual misoprostol given the more favourable safety profile in a hypotensive patient (RANZCOG/state PPH guidelines). However, ergometrine is the most commonly cited second-line uterotonic when no contraindication, and may be appropriate here.

options.text: IM carboprost (15-methyl PGF2alpha) 250 mcg, repeatable up to 8 doses
options.rationale: Australian PPH algorithms (NSW Health, KEMH, RANZCOG) escalate as: oxytocin (bolus + infusion) -> ergometrine (if not hypertensive) -> carboprost 250 mcg IM every 15 minutes (max 2 mg) -> misoprostol 800-1000 mcg PR. In this hypotensive, tachycardic, multiparous patient where ergometrine is relatively contraindicated by haemodynamic instability and uncertain BP trajectory, carboprost is the preferred next-line uterotonic, with tranexamic acid 1 g IV in parallel (RANZCOG PPH; NSW Health PPH).

options.text: Misoprostol 200 mcg PO
options.rationale: Misoprostol may be used as adjunctive uterotonic but the recommended dose is 800-1000 mcg PR/SL, not 200 mcg PO, and it is later in the escalation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV only, with no further uterotonic
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is recommended early in PPH (WOMAN trial; given within 3 hours), but should be given in addition to ongoing uterotonics, not instead of them (RANZCOG).

options.text: Recombinant factor VIIa 90 mcg/kg IV
options.rationale: Recombinant FVIIa is a last-resort haemostatic agent in massive PPH refractory to standard measures; it is not a uterotonic and is not appropriate at this stage (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Australian PPH pharmacological escalation: oxytocin (10 IU IM or 5 IU IV bolus then 40 IU in 1 L over 4 h) -> ergometrine 250 mcg IM/IV if no hypertension -> carboprost (15-methyl PGF2alpha) 250 mcg IM every 15 minutes up to 2 mg -> misoprostol 800-1000 mcg PR. Add tranexamic acid 1 g IV early and consider mechanical/surgical measures if not controlled.
key_points: Always work the 4Ts: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of birth reduces death due to bleeding (WOMAN trial)
Massive transfusion protocol when >=1500 mL or ongoing brisk loss
Mechanical: bimanual compression, Bakri balloon, aortic compression
Surgical: B-Lynch, uterine/internal iliac artery ligation, hysterectomy
explanation.pearls: In a hypotensive woman, avoid ergometrine; choose carboprost (caution in asthma) next.

sources.label: NSW Health: Maternity - prevention, early recognition and management of PPH PD2021_007
WOMAN trial - tranexamic acid in PPH

stem: A 38-year-old G3P3 woman attends her general practice for contraceptive counselling. Her youngest child is 4 months old and exclusively breastfed. Her BMI is 33. She has migraine with aura (typical scintillating scotoma followed by unilateral headache, average twice per month) since age 19. She had a left ankle ORIF 6 weeks ago for a complex fracture and remains in a removable boot, mobilising with crutches; her physiotherapist plans to discontinue the boot in 2 weeks. Her mother had a provoked DVT on the combined oral contraceptive at age 25. She is planning a long-haul flight (16 hours) in two weeks. She is normotensive (118/74) and a non-smoker. She had a Caesarean for breech with her first child and two subsequent uneventful vaginal births. Her cervical screening test is up to date and negative. She has no liver disease, no breast cancer history, no current pelvic infection and no abnormal uterine bleeding. She is open to any contraceptive method that is safe in her current circumstances.

data_table.Examination: Well. Left ankle in boot. No breast lumps. No pelvic tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening up to date negative. No recent FBC.

lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate at this consultation?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill (oestrogen-containing)
options.rationale: COCP is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable risk) for migraine with aura at any age, and additionally UKMEC 3-4 with recent immobilisation post-orthopaedic surgery, BMI >=35 (here 33, but elevated baseline VTE), family history of VTE, age >=35 with multiple risk factors, and breastfeeding before 6 weeks postpartum (now 4 months so less restrictive but cumulative risk is high) (FSRH UKMEC 2016 + 2024 updates).

options.text: Combined transdermal contraceptive patch
options.rationale: Same systemic VTE/arterial risks as COCP; UKMEC 4 for migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena)
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1-2 in all of her stated risk categories (migraine with aura UKMEC 2, postpartum >=4 weeks UKMEC 1, VTE risk UKMEC 2, BMI 33 UKMEC 1) and provides highly effective long-term contraception suitable for breastfeeding and immobilisation; this is the most appropriate choice (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).
source_refs: Family Planning Australia - LARC in special circumstances

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA) IM every 12 weeks
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 2 for migraine with aura and for breastfeeding but is UKMEC 3 in women with multiple VTE risk factors (recent surgery, immobilisation); LARC options with no VTE signal (IUDs, implant) are preferable (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (desogestrel)
options.rationale: POP is safe (UKMEC 1-2) in her circumstances but is less effective (typical-use failure approximately 9%) than LNG-IUS and depends heavily on adherence; in a woman with this risk profile a LARC is preferable (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

explanation.summary: This woman has multiple UKMEC 3/4 considerations for oestrogen-containing contraception: migraine with aura (UKMEC 4 absolute), recent orthopaedic surgery with reduced mobility (UKMEC 3), family history of VTE (UKMEC 3 if first-degree relative <45), planned long-haul flight (additional risk), age 38, BMI 33. The safest and most effective option is the LNG-IUS, which is UKMEC 1-2 across all her risk factors and offers 8 years of contraception.
key_points: Migraine with aura = UKMEC 4 for any combined hormonal contraception (any age)
Recent surgery with immobilisation = UKMEC 3-4 for CHC
BMI >=35 = UKMEC 3 for CHC
First-degree relative VTE <45 = UKMEC 3 for CHC
LNG-IUS and Cu-IUD are UKMEC 1-2 in nearly all medical contexts; insertion postpartum after 4 weeks is straightforward
explanation.pearls: Stack the UKMEC scores. Any one '4' rules out a method; combinations of '3's should push you to a different category entirely.

sources.label: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)
Family Planning Australia: Contraception in special circumstances

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman in established labour at 40 weeks and 2 days gestation is being monitored by continuous CTG in the birth suite. Her previous birth was a normal vaginal delivery. The pregnancy was uncomplicated. Her GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 22 hours ago with clear liquor; she has been in established labour for 9 hours. Cervical examination 30 minutes ago: 8 cm, fully effaced, station 0, position OA, intact caput. She is on no antibiotics. Over the past 90 minutes the midwife has documented a rising maternal temperature (now 38.2 degC), maternal tachycardia (HR 118), uterine tenderness between contractions, and new offensive vaginal discharge. The liquor draining now is meconium-stained (thick, particulate). Her epidural is functioning well. There are no antenatal risk factors for placental insufficiency. She is hydrated and her last paracetamol was 6 hours ago. The neonatal team and obstetric registrar have been called for review of the CTG.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 108/68, HR 118, T 38.2 degC, RR 20, SpO2 98%.
data_table.Examination: Tender uterus between contractions. Offensive thick meconium-stained liquor. Cervix 8 cm, station 0.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 116, WCC 18.2, CRP 86. CTG: baseline 175 bpm (was 145 four hours ago), variability 3-5 bpm (reduced), no accelerations, recurrent late decelerations with each of the last 6 contractions, depth 40 bpm, slow recovery; contractions 5 in 10, lasting 70 seconds.

lead_in: Using RANZCOG Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance (IFM) classification, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue to observe, repeat VE in 2 hours, reassess CTG
options.rationale: The CTG is pathological (RANZCOG IFM: baseline tachycardia, reduced variability, recurrent late decelerations, plus clinical chorioamnionitis and meconium); continued observation alone is unsafe (RANZCOG IFM).

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin augmentation to expedite delivery
options.rationale: Tachysystole and uterine hyperstimulation worsen fetal compromise; augmentation is contraindicated in the presence of pathological CTG and likely chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG IFM).

options.text: Insert fetal scalp electrode and consider fetal blood sampling
options.rationale: FBS may have a role in pathological CTG with intact membranes and no other red flags, but in the presence of clinical chorioamnionitis, meconium, prolonged ROM, and recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability, the appropriate action is delivery, not further sampling (RANZCOG IFM).

options.text: Stop oxytocin if running, position left lateral, give IV fluid bolus, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, and arrange Category 1 Caesarean section
options.rationale: Intrauterine resuscitation (positioning, fluids, stopping any uterotonic, considering tocolysis) and IV antibiotics for chorioamnionitis are correct, but at 8 cm in a multiparous woman with progressing labour and persistent pathological CTG plus chorioamnionitis, the operative-vaginal vs Cat 1 CS decision depends on station and head position; CS is reasonable but the most appropriate next step is intrauterine resuscitation, treat chorioamnionitis, and prepare for expedited delivery by the most appropriate route - this option captures most actions but pre-empts a CS at 8 cm without resuscitation trial.

options.text: Intrauterine resuscitation (stop oxytocin if running, left lateral position, IV fluid bolus, consider tocolysis), commence IV antibiotics for chorioamnionitis, paracetamol for maternal pyrexia, and prepare for expedited birth with senior obstetric review for mode and timing
options.rationale: The combined features (pathological CTG, clinical chorioamnionitis, thick meconium, prolonged ROM 22 hours, maternal tachycardia and pyrexia) require simultaneous intrauterine resuscitation, treatment of the underlying chorioamnionitis with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, antipyretics, and senior decision-making for expedited birth (vaginal vs Cat 1 CS depending on response and progress); this is the RANZCOG IFM-aligned response (RANZCOG IFM; RANZCOG chorioamnionitis).

explanation.summary: This is a pathological RANZCOG IFM CTG (baseline tachycardia, reduced variability, recurrent late decelerations) on a background of clinical chorioamnionitis (maternal fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, offensive discharge), thick meconium, and prolonged ROM. Required actions: intrauterine resuscitation, IV antibiotics, maternal cooling/paracetamol, and senior-led plan for expedited birth.
key_points: Pathological CTG features: baseline <100 or >160 with reduced variability, recurrent late or prolonged decelerations, sinusoidal pattern
Maternal pyrexia raises fetal baseline; treat the source and the symptom
Intrauterine resuscitation acronym (SPOIL): Stop oxytocin, Position left lateral, Oxygen (no longer routine), IV fluids, Lower BP if iatrogenic (e.g. epidural top-up), tocolysis if hyperstimulation
Chorioamnionitis: empirical IV antibiotics (e.g. amoxicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole per local policy)
Mode of birth depends on station, position, progress and response to resuscitation
explanation.pearls: Maternal fever raises fetal baseline by ~10 bpm per degree; treat the fever, treat the infection, resuscitate the uterus, then make a delivery plan.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance (IFM) Clinical Guideline 2019
